body_txt: One quick way to establish conflict for a male lead (and this trope is almost Always Male ) is to make him a divorced or separated father. The story will usually open with the divorce or the immediate aftermath, with the children of that marriage old enough to resent Dad for "leaving them". The mother invariably keeps the house and has primary if not full custody of the kids, so the story opens with our hero left out in the cold. It's generally implied that the relationship ended when Mom had enough of Dad's misdemeanors: the ex may be snarky, resentful or exasperated in their interactions, but neither the lead nor usually the children will act as if she were the problem. But this usually does not turn audience sympathy away from the male lead: she frequently comes off as seriously insensitive or unforgiving, while he seems like a victim of circumstance. And there will be a new guy in the picture: Mom's second husband, fiancé, or fairly serious boyfriend. Since she has custody of the kids, the First Dad will be worried the Step Dad will win them over because he has more time with them and he's handsomer, more successful, more empathetic and an all around better dad (though a painfully dull and "safe" rebound guy is also possible). The kids, usually a boy and a girl, will have one of four relationships with the First Dad and Step Dad: passive aggressiveness, wary distance, civil cohabitation, or a loving and respectful rapport. For variety and contrast, they usually each have a different relationship with the dad and step dad. There are two variants: Contrast Parent with New Paramour , where we usually root for the stepdad who's trying to win over the kids (in these stories, there usually isn't a First Dad around to hog the sympathy). If the Kids Play Matchmaker , they may Invoke this trope to pair their mother back up with their father.
